back_translation: He began to teach beside the sea again, and an enormous crowd gathered around Him, so that He had to embark on a boat and be seated in the midst of the sea, while the whole crowd stood on the shore beside the sea.
He taught them many things in parables, and in His teaching, He said:
'Listen carefully! Behold, a certain farmer went out to sow seed.
While he was sowing, some seed fell along the path, and the birds came and pecked and ate it all up.
Some fell on rocky ground, where there was little soil, and it sprouted up immediately because the soil was shallow.
But when the sun rose, the young sprouts were scorched, and because they had no root, they withered and dried up.
Some fell in a thornbush; the thornbush grew up and covered over the seedlings, and it turned out they bore no fruit at all.
And some fell in good soil, sprouted and grew vigorously and bore fruit — some thirtyfold, some sixtyfold, some a hundredfold.'
Then He said: 'Whoever has ears for hearing, let him listen.'
When He was alone, the disciples who were around Him, together with the twelve disciples, asked Him about those parables.
He said to them: 'The hidden-depth of the kingdom of God has been graciously given to you, but for outsiders, everything is in parables,
so that "when they look, they keep looking but do not see, and when they hear, they hear but do not understand, lest they repent and receive forgiveness."'
He said to them: 'Do you still not understand this parable? Then how will you understand all the parables?'
The sower sows the sacred Word.
These people are the seed that falls along the path, where the sacred Word is sown. When they hear it, Satan comes and immediately takes the sacred Word that was sown in them away.
In a similar way, these people are the seed sown on rocky ground. When they hear the sacred Word, they receive it with joy immediately.
But they have no root in themselves, so they endure only for a little while. When hardship or persecution arises because of the sacred Word, they immediately stumble and fall.
And others are the seed sown in the thornbush — they are the ones who hear the sacred Word,
but the worries of this age, the deception of wealth, and the desires for other things enter in and cover over the sacred Word, and it bears no fruit.
But those are the seed sown in good soil — the ones who hear the sacred Word, receive it, and bear fruit — some thirtyfold, some sixtyfold, some a hundredfold.'
Then He went on to say to them: 'Would anyone bring a lamp to place it under a container, or under a bed? Isn't it to be set up on a lampstand?
For there is nothing hidden except for the purpose of being revealed, and nothing concealed except for the purpose of coming out into the open.
If anyone has ears for hearing, let him listen.'
He continued speaking to them: 'Carefully examine what you hear. With whatever measure you measure out, it will be measured out to you with that measure, and you will receive even more.
For whoever has, that person will be given even more. But whoever does not have, even what he has will be taken away from him.'
He continued to say: 'The kingdom of God is like this — like a person who sows seed down on the soil,
who sometimes sleeps and sometimes wakes, through night and day, while the seed sprouts and grows without him himself knowing how it happens.
The earth bears fruit on its own, automatically — first a young sprout, then an ear of grain, then full kernels within the ear.
And when the fruit is ripe and ready, he immediately uses the sickle to reap, because it is the time of harvest.'
He continued: 'With what shall we compare the kingdom of God? Or what parable shall we use to explain this kingdom?
It is like a mustard seed, which when sown in the soil is the smallest among all the seeds in the world,
but when it has been sown, it grows up and becomes the largest among all the garden plants, putting forth huge branches, so that the birds of the air can build nests under its shade.'
He spoke the sacred Word to them with many such parables, according to what they were able to hear and understand.
He did not speak to them at all without using a parable, but when He was alone with His disciples, He explained everything to them.
On that day, when evening came, He said to them: 'Let us cross over to the other side.'
They dismissed the crowd and took Him along, in the condition where He was already in the boat, and there were other boats following His royal journey as well.
A violent windstorm arose, and the waves crashed into the boat, so that the boat was nearly sinking already.
But He Himself was sleeping in royal repose at the stern on a certain cushion. They woke Him up and said to Him: 'Teacher, are You not concerned that we Your servants are about to perish?'
He woke up, royally charged the wind, and said to the sea: 'Be silent! Be still!' The wind stopped, and the sea became extremely calm and smooth.
Then He said to them: 'Why are you so cowardly? Do you still have no faith?'
They were filled with great awe, and said to one another: 'Who then is this person? For even the wind and the sea obey Him.'
